sentences.text: Since, uh-- by the end of '42 till 1944, in October.
Right.
And when the ghetto was liquidated, he left.
He was taken to Plaszow.
My brother and sister and his two children, with some other people, were hiding in a hiding place in the ghetto.
Because they had-- they did not have papers to go to Plaszow.
Right.
They took the young children away.
So, after one week, my brother and my sister heard that there was no more shooting in the ghetto.
It's quiet, so they think it was safe to run-- to go out.
My younger brother went out before the liquidation.
A day or two before, he ran out of the ghetto, [NON-ENGLISH].
He was working on the barges in the Vistula.
And after 10 days, my uncle's two children, Esther and [NON-ENGLISH] were in the hidden place.
And he was afraid that they, maybe they will run out of food.
So he went to Amon Goeth, and he said that he has machineries in the ghetto, because he had a factory of the [? brothers ?].
And he put them in boxes, pretending that it's machineries.
And he took them to Plaszow.
And he hid them in Plaszow from 1943, end of March, to 1944, when Mengele came to take over the children from Plaszow.
Trying to escape from the ghetto.
Right.
Plaszow was a camp that there wasn't a day that somebody should not die.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some nights we would stay in the appellplatz.
And mostly tall people-- Amon Goeth would walk over, put the gun behind his ear, and shoot him for no reason at all.
No, that was the only time that I saw him.
In May of 1944.
On a Sunday.
It was in Mother's Day.
They was playing music-- [GERMAN].
'Mother, Mother, I will return.' When they took away the children, there was a lot of screaming.
Throughout the appellplatz.
In 1939, or beginning of '40, Oskar Schindler received, as a Jewish enterprise, received Emalia.
But he was immediately different than the other Germans.
Normally, the Germans will take away the Jewish stores.
And people will have, next to the store, a nice apartment or a nice house.
They will take that away and chase the Jew out.
He-- because, maybe, it was a factory-- he said, I don't know how to run pots and pans.
So he took in the Jewish people to run it for him.
And he immediately made a 50-50 deal. 50% of the goods that there were made was going to the German government, and 50% was sold on the market-- black market.
And on the black market was also 50-50. 25-- he took 50% for himself, so he will have to bribe people to run parties.
And 50% he will leave  it in the Jewish hands, and they will buy food.
In the beginning, in the Emalia was working Christians.
But because of being able to get a Kennkarte-- Kennkarte, that's a special paper to go to the camp, to the, to the ghetto.
You had to have papers that you work in the Emalia.
Like I said before, German enterprises.
So people will be in the ghetto, and from the ghetto they will go out to the Emalia to work.
People will pay money, gold, silver, whatever they had-- could lay a hand on them.
Even they will assign houses for the future, those red houses-- to get him papers that he works in Emalia.
In 1943, when they liquidated the ghetto, that time was Emalia even more important, because, instead of going to Plaszow to concentration camp, Oskar Schindler built barracks.
And he made his own camp.
And in that camp, nobody beat you.
You were not hungry.
You were treated half-decent.
In 1944, Poles start run-- the Russians start taking over Poland.
They were already in Tarnow, next large town to Krakow.
And he had orders to liquidate his Emalia factory.
There was a Madritsch who was making uniforms.
He also had order to liquidate his factory.
And Madritsch-- he wanted to do something with Madritsch.
But Madritsch said, I did as much as I could.
And he washed his hands.
And the good part of Oskar Schindler-- the outstanding thing-- is that in August-- he was a very rich man.
He had a lot of money from the black market, and from presents that he got from people to let them in the Emalia.
That he could have said, I'm going to take my wife, and maybe a girlfriend, and go to Switzerland or go to Germany-- deep in Germany.
And I did for the Jews as much as I could.
But he didn't do that.
He was determined to save the Jews-- at least his Jews.
So he went to Brunnlitz.
It  was a bombed out factory.
And he went to Berlin.

And he told them in Berlin that, if they let him have his people-- that they are engineers, sheet metal workers, and all good mechanics-- if they let him rebuild this factory, he going to be able  in a few months to make ammunition for the Germans.
But in '44, in August, the factory was not ready.
And the papers did not come through from Berlin.
So the people from-- who were in the Emalia were taken into Plaszow.
The women were in Plaszow.
And most of the men from the Emalia-- not all of them, but some of them-- most of them-- they put on a train in Plaszow, and they shipped them to Mauthausen.
That time, the train was standing-- the boxcar.
There are no engines, so the boxcars were standing for two or three days, without water.
And it was-- the people almost suffocated.
So Schindler found out about it.
He came into Plaszow.
And he gave Goeth some presents.
And he said, I guarantee you that the people will not run away.
Open up the, the doors.
Give them some air.
And let them cook some soup.
Give them some soup.
Give them something to eat.
They are good material for the military.
They are professionals, good skilled people.
And orders came out in the kitchen to cook some food.
And myself, with some other boys, we took the food down.
And after we finished with the food, Oskar Schindler told us to lay not fire hoses but water hoses.
He himself was dressed up like-- he was a personalty like our President Ford.
And for the first time I heard about Schindler.

But I met Oskar Schindler, and he was saying 'mach schnell.' And we were pouring water-- hose down the-- and I met people in Florida, and they told me that we didn't do them a favor, because it steamed.
But he meant well.
And then I said to myself, who can that German be?
Is it possible that-- I saw a German Hakenkreuz in civilian clothes?
And his talk, his personality, was entirely different than other Germans.
Little did I know that in November-- in October I was put on a train, because they started liquidating Plaszow.
And I was put to Gross-Rosen.
In Gross-Rosen, when we got to Gross-Rosen, there were no beds.
We sat on the floor in clutches.
If one guy had to go to the bathroom, the whole row had to-- that's how we were, like sardines.
And we were taken-- we were getting showers.
We were getting new clothing.
They took us-- all our old clothing away, because they were thinking maybe we have some stuff sewed in-- maybe some, some money, some valuables.
And we were told that they don't keep people more than five days.
If, during the five days in Gross-Rosen, if they don't call you out to, to some camp to work, or some factory, they liquidate you.
They kill you.
And after three days, rumors had it that Oskar Schindler is coming with a list.
And we were thinking it's wishful thinking, or maybe somebody dreams up a story.
On the fourth day, we were called out on the appellplatz.
And we were told, every name that will be called, to go to the side.
And one of the Jewish police, Marcel Goldberg, was reading the, the list.
At number 205, they called out 'Puntierer Mejzesz.'
And the Germans were yelling, 'Beruf?
Beruf?' What's my trade?
I didn't know what to say.
But somebody else who was with Marcel Goldberg was saying 'sheet metal worker.' Till this day, I don't know who put me on the list and who put town sheet metal worker.
But I was 19 years old then.
I already was a few years in camp.
I was a kid of 14 years old when the war broke out.
I had no opportunity to learn a trade.
But somebody lied for me so I can survive.
And I went to Brunnlitz.
And in Brunnlitz-- and sometimes I'm ashamed to say to my fellow people who were in the Mauthausen, or Dachau, or other camp that I was in Brunnlitz.
Comparing, it was like a summer camp.
And Oskar Schindler spent all his money to buy food, because he didn't produce anything.
So he didn't get no money from the German people.
And one episode, I have to talk to you about it.
In February of 1945, somebody from the railroad station-- a Czech-- called into the office in Brunnlitz.
The guy's name was-- he was a secretary-- Pemper.
That there's a train, just carloads without an engine.
They took the engine out so they will pull out the people-- their own people from Czechoslovakia-- into the American zone.
They all were screaming at the Germans, 'Amerikaner zone.' Because they knew that the espionage were working that good, that by the Americans they will have the best POWs.
And that people are crying.
And they sit on the siding to die.
If we will take them into the camp.
And he asks Mrs.
Schindler, because Oskar Schindler wasn't there at that time.
He always went with a truck with a few people to get some food for us, some bread, some--
So she said, by all means, bring them in.
So they brought in about six, seven carloads of people.
Some of them were complete frozen to death.
We worked-- myself with some friends-- with picks and shovels.
And we finally open up the doors.
When we open up the doors, we think we did the people a favor.
And one was grabbing by the legs.
And the other one was grabbing by the shoulders and by the arms.
And we tried to lift them up.
But their behind-- excuse me for the-- but that's the only way I can describe it-- were frozen.
And the screaming was unbearable.
So we made a fire, because when we throw cold water it wouldn't, it will freeze it.
That how cold it was.
So we will hold our fingers.
And we will throw some hot water, so we wouldn't burn them.
And we pulled out about 30 men.
Out of these 30, a few did not survive.
But 25 brought out-- 100%, in another day or two, they would have been frozen to death.
And Mrs.
Schindler was cooking cereals for them.
And she would ask us boys to carry it in, because inside, they couldn't move.
So the smell was unbearable.
But she will cook cereal from time to time and feed it to those people.
And, of course, the last day before the end of the war, he took us in in the factory.
He closed the doors.
He didn't let the Germans go in.
Said, I only will talk to my Jewish-- And he told us, the war is ending.
I have a storage with guns.
I have a storage with some material.
I want everyone who knows how to use a gun to get the guns.
And everybody should get a package of too-- of clothes for a suit, for a coat, for shirts, shoes, so you will be dressed up like human beings.
And if in case the Germans would want to kill you, put up a fight.
They may run away.
They may not.
I, myself, cannot stay in Czechoslovakia, for a reason that I cannot tell you.
Because in Czechoslovakia he was condemned to death.
He had a death warrant for him.
And he took a truck with about 10 guys and his wife.
I don't know if he had the girlfriend there or not.
And he went-- he was dressed up like one of the--
Jews-- uh-- and he escaped to Germany.
He was caught up in-- the Americans-- because the Americans were very close.
They already passed Linz, in Austria.
No, Zuckerman went to Mauthausen.
Zuckerman was in the Emalia.
And from the Emalia he was taken to Plaszow.
From Plaszow he was taken to Emalia.
When he came to camp Plaszow, he came from other camps-- from [NON-ENGLISH].
And they needed 150 people.
And somehow he was among them that went to Emalia.
And from the Emalia went to Plaszow to a train.
Maybe he was in Plaszow for a few days.
And he was taken to Mauthausen.
I was with an uncle, Uncle Isaac.
He was in Plaszow from the liquidation of the ghetto, from March '43 till October.
And he was also put on the list, because he had a factory of metal.
So he was with me in Brunnlitz.
There was another uncle.
But he went also to Emalia.
And from Emalia he went to Mauthausen.
Yes.
I can swear to you, not one single piece of ammunition went out.
And we was-- the only scare that we have in Brunnlitz, that he's going to be caught, because every few weeks, some inspectors came.
And we said to each other, here we go.
They're going to shoot us all.
But he will take them to his villa, get them drunk, give them some packages.
And they will go 'Heil Hitler.' And they will went away.
We were very lucky that the war finished in May.
Hadn't the war finished, who knows what would have happened?
He would have been caught sooner or later.
But, just to clarify, what would he have been caught doing?
What was, what was, what was going on in that factory?
Some of us baked matzohs for Pesach.
Can I-- have to tell you more?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we [INAUDIBLE].
And we-- He would go into the factory and have two packs of cigarettes.
Lit up a cigarette and put it on the machine and leave it.
And the Jewish fellow was dying for that cigarette.
He would leave-- I can say to you, if I would be a fanatic, I would say that the angel flew down to Gross-Rosen, wrote down my name.
And Oskar Schindler was sent from God.
But maybe someday we will find out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe the skies are not empty after all.
There was no ammunition.
They claim, some people claim, that he once bought a carload of hand grenade from someplace, brought it in from a factory.
And he said that this factory was producing it.
But our factory did not produce not one single piece of ammunition.
Nothing.
Almost-- no, no-- he was saying-- the factory-- the machineries are not working yet.
It was a bombed-out factory.
But the machineries are still put to work.
He's working with the people.
And then, in December-- we got in in October-- the end of October.
In December, he went to Auschwitz, himself.
And he insist on the 300 women-- the same women who worked in the Emalia-- that these 300 women, he needs them.
And they came in in December.
It was a holiday, because most wives, sisters, kids, mothers were among the 300 women.
And they for sure didn't do nothing.
He was getting someplace wool.
And they were knitting sweaters for their boyfriends, for the relatives-- and gloves and ear mufflers.
The woman didn't do absolutely nothing.
And he  got them.
He came with them back.
They want to give him some other women.
He wouldn't take it.
[DRINKS]
There were guards.
The was a guy, Poldek--
Yes. --who was shot in the foot.
He was, uh, wounded.
He, he tried to make all kind of troubles.
But he always talked to him.
And he told him, they are my workers.
And I'm going to go to hierarchy.
He always threatened him to go to Berlin.
And-- One time, I saw-- a boy was-- a friend of mine was stealing potatoes.
And-- in Brunnlitz.
And a German from the guards put a potato in his mouth.
And he put down 'I'm a tom-- a potato thief.' Schindler came in.
He immediately said to him to take it off.
This is not a sadistic camp.
And you treat people right.
He's a worker.
And they, they-- either he was bluffing, or he had-- he was, a few times, in trouble.
But he always had somebody in the high Gestapo, or maybe the highest-ranking Gestapo, that he can always wiggle himself out.
We fixed it.
We fixed the roofs.
And we fixed the machineries.
There was some machineries there.
So there was people there to fix the machines.
So the [? stanza ?], the-- everything on the machinery needs-- One day, he said he hasn't got raw material.
How he talked to the German, how he got away with it, I have no idea.
But the fact is that everybody who walked in in that camp walked out, except one girl who died on tuberculosis.
And he bought from-- for her a grave.
And we made a Jewish burial during the time-- I think it was in January or February.
This is unheard or by the Germans and in a camp.
Not in Brunnlitz.
Out of the camp.
Out of the factory.
It was a villa.
He lived in the villa.
Over 1,100.
Not too many.
But older people were a lot.
